coredata.dc:title: Cyclooxygenase-2-targeting fluorescent carbon dots for the selective imaging of Golgi apparatus
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xin
Chen, Lin
Wei, Ying-Ying
Du, Jing-Lei
Yu, Shi-Ping
Liu, Xu-Guang
Liu, Wei
Liu, Yan-Jun
Yang, Yong-Zhen
Li, Qiang
coredata.dc:description: Golgi apparatus is an important organelle responsible for transporting proteins in cells. Accurate monitoring of Golgi apparatus status by targeting fluorescence imaging technology is of great significance for visualization of Golgi apparatus' physiological status. Cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2) is a protease on the Golgi apparatus surface of cells. By means of hydrogen bonding between COX-2 and sulfonamide groups, orange-emitting Golgi apparatus-targeting carbon dots (GTCDs) were developed for the efficient imaging monitoring of Golgi apparatus. GTCDs were synthesized from p-phenylenediamine and benzene sulfonamide by one-step solvothermal methods. GTCDs had uniform size (∼3.36 nm), graphitized structure, maximum emission wavelength of 612 nm, low cytotoxicity, 24 h metabolic time, and good photostability. In addition, GTCDs had an excellent Golgi apparatus-targeting effect (average Pearson's co-localization coefficient = 0.92) and ultrafast imaging capability of Golgi apparatus (imaging immediately after the addition of GTCDs). The selective targeting of GTCDs to Golgi apparatus was attributed to the binding of the sulfonamide moiety on the surface of GTCDs to the active pocket site of COX-2 in Golgi apparatus through hydrogen bonds, which led to the localization of GTCDs to Golgi apparatus. It is believed that GTCDs have great potential in the fields of precise organelle imaging and targeting drug delivery as Golgi apparatus-targeting biological fluorescent probes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Golgi apparatus targeting
Cyclooxygenase-2
Carbon dots
Ultrafast fluorescence imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ultrafast Golgi apparatus imaging by GTCDs

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research is supported by the following research projects: Shanxi-Zheda Institute of Advanced Materials and Chemical Engineering, China ( 2021SX-FR010 ); Shanxi Technology Innovation Center for Controlled and Sustained Release of Nano-drugs, China ( 202104010911026 ); National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51803148 ); Central Guiding Local Science and Technology Development Fund, China ( YDZX20201400001722 ); the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province, China ( 201901D211501 , 201901D211502 , and 201901D111419 ); and Postgraduate Innovation Project of Shanxi Province, China ( 2021Y266 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structure and composition characterization of GTCDs. (a) TEM images, insets are particle size statistical histograms (top left), HRTEM images (top right); and GTCD aqueous solution photos (bottom right) taken in sunlight (left) and UV light (right); (b) Raman spectra; (c) Zeta potential; (d) FT-IR spectra; (e) full XPS spectra of GTCDs; and High-resolution XPS peaks of (f) Cls, (g) N1s, (h) Ols, and (i) S2p.

Optical properties of GTCDs. (a) UV–Vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra at 465 nm; (b) Emission spectra of GTCDs with different excitation wavelengths; (c) Fluorescence intensity changes of GTCDs under continuous irradiation for 60 min; and (d) Fluorescence spectra of GTCDs at different pH values. The inset indicates the change of fluorescence intensity.
Optical and ex-vivo fluorescence imaging of BALB/c mice organs, including heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney at different treating times of GTCDs (λex/em = 430/620 nm).
Histopathological analysis of GTCD treatment for 24 h. (a) Heart; (b) Liver; (c) Spleen; (d) Lung; and (e) Kidneys. Scale bar: 50 μm.

Golgi apparatus targeting ability of GTCDs and CGCDs in HeLa cells. (a, f) Bright field images; (b, g) Fluorescence image of NBD co-incubated with HeLa cells; Fluorescence images of (c) GTCDs and (h) CGCDs in HeLa cells; (d) Merged image of (a–c); (i) Merged image of (f–h); and (e, j) Co-located scatter plot. Scale bar: 10 μm. (λex/em = 458/580–700 nm).

Fluorescence images of (a) PDMPO (λex/em = 488/520–570 nm), (e) NBD (λex/em = 488/520–560 nm), (i) DAPI (λex/em = 358/420–500 nm), (b, f) GTCDs (λex/em = 553/611–663 nm) in VX2 cells, and (j) GTCDs in HepG2 cells; (c, g, k) Merged images; (d, h, l) Intensity scatter plot. Scale bar: 60 μm.
Fluorescence images of GTCDs co-incubated with HeLa for 0, 10, 100, and 200 min (λex/em = 458/580–640 nm). Scale bar: 20 μm.
(a) Chemical formula of benzene sulfonamide; (b) Chemical formula of p-toluene sulfonic acid; and (c) 3D and (d) 2D diagrams of the binding site of benzene sulfonamide with COX-2.
(a) Structure of GTCDs; (b) Docking site of GTCDs and COX-2; (c)Binding mode of GTCDs and COX-2; and (d) Binding mode of CGCDs and COX-2.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis and biological characterization procedure of GTCDs.
The docking results of different molecules with COX-2 enzyme by AutoDock.

head.ce:title: Cyclooxygenase-2-targeting fluorescent carbon dots for the selective imaging of Golgi apparatus

International Co-laboratory of Medical Epigenetics and Metabolism (Ministry of Science and Technology)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Medical Epigenetics, International Co-laboratory of Medical Epigenetics and Metabolism (Ministry of Science and Technology), Institutes of Biomedical Sciences, Fudan University, Shanghai, 200032, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Golgi apparatus is an important organelle responsible for transporting proteins in cells. Accurate monitoring of Golgi apparatus status by targeting fluorescence imaging technology is of great significance for visualization of Golgi apparatus' physiological status. Cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2) is a protease on the Golgi apparatus surface of cells. By means of hydrogen bonding between COX-2 and sulfonamide groups, orange-emitting Golgi apparatus-targeting carbon dots (GTCDs) were developed for the efficient imaging monitoring of Golgi apparatus. GTCDs were synthesized from p-phenylenediamine and benzene sulfonamide by one-step solvothermal methods. GTCDs had uniform size (∼3.36 nm), graphitized structure, maximum emission wavelength of 612 nm, low cytotoxicity, 24 h metabolic time, and good photostability. In addition, GTCDs had an excellent Golgi apparatus-targeting effect (average Pearson's co-localization coefficient = 0.92) and ultrafast imaging capability of Golgi apparatus (imaging immediately after the addition of GTCDs). The selective targeting of GTCDs to Golgi apparatus was attributed to the binding of the sulfonamide moiety on the surface of GTCDs to the active pocket site of COX-2 in Golgi apparatus through hydrogen bonds, which led to the localization of GTCDs to Golgi apparatus. It is believed that GTCDs have great potential in the fields of precise organelle imaging and targeting drug delivery as Golgi apparatus-targeting biological fluorescent probes.

Introducing sulfonamide groups into carbon dots, and designing orange-emitting Golgi apparatus-targeting carbon dots (GTCDs) based on the ligand-receptor binding.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Golgi apparatus is a key structure for transporting and secreting proteins/enzymes in cells. As an important part of a cell, subtle pH, electrical, or morphological change in Golgi apparatus will disrupt the biological system and lead to organ damage, such as eye, kidney, and liver diseases [1–4]. In addition, Golgi apparatus shows higher levels of proteins or enzymes in prostate cancer than normal cells or tissues, making it a therapeutic target for prostate cancer [5,6]. Therefore, targeting fluorescence imaging of Golgi apparatus is paramount for monitoring its microstructure and morphology as well as imaging treatment of cancer. As a new type of targeting fluorescent probe, carbon dots (CDs) exhibit a variety of advantages, including small size (<10 nm), outstanding photoluminescence, excellent biocompatibility, high water-solubility, and good photochemical stability [7,8]. The abundant functional groups on their surface, such as amino and carboxyl groups, facilitate their modification for biological coupling. Therefore, they have attracted more and more attention in the field of Golgi apparatus fluorescence imaging.

However, owing to the lack of targeting ligands, most CDs are not selective when they are internalized in cells and cannot target individual organelles such as Golgi apparatus for imaging. Therefore, by combining the good fluorescence and easy modification of CDs with the targeting of ligands, it is expected to achieve Golgi apparatus-targeting CDs (GTCDs) for the precise observation of Golgi apparatus. Currently, some researchers have carried out this work [9,10]. For example, Hou et al. synthesized GTCDs by the retention strategy of basic ligands in acidic organelles [11]. However, the passive targeting strategy based on the combination of alkaline probe and acidic environment cannot fully explain the specificity of targeting Golgi apparatus, because there may be a targeting effect on other organelles with a similar acidic environment. Therefore, it is urgent to establish a clear Golgi apparatus targeting mechanism in order to clarify the structural relationship and interaction between GTCDs and Golgi apparatus and design GTCD probes through more effective active targeting strategy.

Strategy based on ligand-receptor binding is of great significance for the research and development of Golgi apparatus-targeting imaging fluorescent probes. Cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2) is an excellent traceable biomarker, being found in abundance on Golgi apparatus. Zhang et al. prepared a COX-2 specific molecular probe with benzene sulfonamide moiety to monitor the apoptosis process of Golgi apparatus [12]. Kurumbail et al. found that benzene sulfonamide moiety in COX-2 inhibitor SC-558 can selectively bind to the active site of COX-2 [13]. Therefore, it is expected to introduce sulfonamide groups into CDs, and design GTCDs based on the ligand-receptor binding between sulfonamide groups of benzene sulfonamide and COX-2 protease and the excellent photoluminescence performance of CDs.

In this work, a novel strategy for the preparation of orange-emitting GTCDs was demonstrated. With COX-2 as the imaging target and p-phenylenediamine and benzene sulfonamide as precursors, GTCDs with rapid and accurate imaging of Golgi apparatus were prepared by one-step solvothermal method (Scheme 1 ). The optimum emission wavelength of the GTCDs in aqueous solution is 612 nm. Co-localization analysis shows the average Pearson coefficient up to 0.92. The probe has good optical performance, low toxicity, and fast and accurately targeting imaging properties for Golgi apparatus. Through AutoDock simulation calculation, it was verified that the sulfonamide group on the surface of GTCDs realizes the targeting effect of GTCDs on Golgi apparatus through hydrogen bonding. The excellent biological properties of GTCDs indicate their potential as a tool to evaluate the physiological and pathological states of Golgi apparatus.

Anhydrous methanol (CH4O) was purchased from Xingfu Science and Technology Development Co. Ltd., Tianjin. p-Phenylenediamine (C6H8N2), benzene sulfonamide (C6H7NO2S), p-toluene sulfonic acid (C7H8O3S), and celecoxib were bought from Maclin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd., Shanghai. Golgi apparatus-targeting imaging agent (NBD C6-ceramide, NBD) was purchased from Zhifan Biotechnology Co. Ltd., Nanjing. Nucleus-targeting imaging agent (4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole, DAPI) was purchased from Boster Biological Technology Co. Ltd., USA. Lysosomal dye (LysoSensor Yellow/Blue DND-160, PDMPO) was purchased from Kaiji Biotechnology Co. Ltd., Nanjing. Cervical cancer cell (HeLa), human skin fibroblasts (HSF), normal liver cells (HL-7702), rabbit tumor cells (VX2), and human hepatocellular carcinomas (HepG2) were provided by Fuheng Biotechnology Co. Ltd., Shanghai. Fetal bovine serum was purchased from Cellmax Cell Technology Co. Ltd., Beijing. Phosphate buffer solution, trypsin, CCK-8 kit, and medium were purchased from Boster Biological Technology Co. Ltd., Wuhan. BALB/c mice and BALB/c nude mice were purchased from Vital River Laboratory Animal Technology Co. Ltd., Beijing. Disposable membrane (0.22 μm) came from Lianghe Glass Instrument Company, Taixing. The dialysis bag (1000 Da) was obtained from Yuansong Instrument Technology Co. Ltd., Shanghai. Super pure water (H2O) was made by our own laboratory.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were taken on an electron microscope with carbon film as supporting substrate (JEM-100CXII, Japan). Raman spectra were recorded on a Raman spectrometer (HORIBA Evolution, Shanghai). The excitation wavelength was 532 nm, the integral time was 20 s, the spot size was 2 μm, and the total power was 12 mW. The zeta potential of GTCDs was measured by dynamic light scattering (Nano-zs90, Malvern, UK). Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FT-IR; TENSOR 27, Germany BRUKER), X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS; Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250 Xi, England) and elemental analysis (EA; Vario EL/micro cube type, Germany) were used to analyze the surface functional groups, chemical bonds, and element content of GTCDs. Fluorescence (FL) spectra and ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were measured on a fluorescence spectrometer (F-4700, Japan; light source: 150 W ozone free xenon lamp) and a spectrophotometer (756 MC UV–Vis, Shanghai, China), respectively. Organelle localization imaging was performed on a laser confocal microscope (LSM 880, Germany). Dynamic images of living cells were taken on a living cell workstation (A1R HD25/A1 HD25, Japan). The mouse in vivo imaging and tissue imaging were taken on a small animal 3D vivo imaging instrument (PerkinElmer, Germany). Pathological analysis was performed on a digital pathological section scanning system (3D Histech Pannoramic SCAN, Hungary).

With p-phenylenediamine as carbon and nitrogen sources and benzene sulfonamide as a target functional group precursor, GTCDs were synthesized by one-step solvothermal method. The specific steps were as follows: first, 0.0003 mol of p-phenylenediamine (0.0324 g) and 0.0001 mol of benzene sulfonamide (0.0157 g) were dissolved in 40 mL of anhydrous methanol to form a transparent solution after 3 min of sonication; then, the solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and sealed for reaction at 453.15 K for 8 h; after naturally cooling to room temperature, the dark wine-red solution of GTCDs was filtered through 0.22 μM hydrophilic microporous filter membrane to remove large particles, and the obtained solution was rotary-evaporated to remove excess methanol, followed by the dialysis of concentrated GTCD solution in ultrapure water with a dialysis bag (MWCO 1000 Da) for 24 h to remove unreacted small molecules; finally, the solution was freeze-dried to obtain dark red powder.

The effect of sulfonamide groups on the targeting of GTCDs was verified by carbon dots (CGCDs) in control trails. p-Toluene sulfonic acid, a precursor similar to benzene sulfonamide, was selected to replace the sulfonamide group on the surface of CDs with benzene sulfonic acid group for control experiment. CGCDs were synthesized by the same method: 0.0003 mol of p-phenylenediamine (0.0324 g) and 0.0001 mol of p-toluene sulfonic acid (0.0172 g) as precursors, were reacted at 453.15 K for 8 h and the same purification steps were carried out to obtain dark red powder. The effect of COX-2 on the targeting of GTCDs was verified by carbon dots (CGCDs-2) in control trails. Celecoxib, a COX-2 inhibitor, was selected for control experiment. CGCDs-2 were synthesized via the same method: taking 0.0003 mol of p-phenylenediamine (0.0324 g) and 0.0001 mol of celecoxib (0.0381 g) as precursors, were reacted at 453.15 K for 8 h and the same purification steps were carried out to obtain black red powder.

The luminescence properties of GTCDs were evaluated. The optimal emission wavelength and excitation wavelength of GTCD aqueous solution (50 μg/mL) were measured by fluorescence spectrometry, and the absorption spectrum of GTCDs was measured by UV–Vis spectrophotometry. The photostability of GTCDs was further tested. First, the pH value of GTCD solution was adjusted with NaOH (1 M) and HCl (1 M), and the fluorescence intensity of GTCD aqueous solution (50 μg/mL) at different pH values (2–13) was measured by fluorescence spectrometry (Ex: 462 nm; Em: 612 nm). Second, the photobleaching resistance of GTCDs was evaluated by monitoring the intensity of emission of GTCD solution (50 μg/mL) under continuous ultraviolet irradiation (365 nm, 6 W, 0–60 min; Ex: 462 nm; Em: 612 nm).

The Golgi apparatus targeting performance of GTCDs was evaluated by laser confocal microscopy. First, HeLa, HSF, HepG2, VX2, and HL-7702 cells were digested and dispersed in 10% fetal bovine serum medium (containing 0.1% antigen, 1% penicillin-streptomycin mixture (double antibody)), and then added to imaging culture dish (1 mL/well) and incubated (310.15 K, 5% CO2) for 24 h. When they grew and covered 80%–90% of bottom, the imaging culture dish was taken out, the supernatant was discarded, and the cells were rinsed with PBS buffer solution for 3 times. Then, they were treated by CDs (GTCD, CGCD, or CGCDs-2 sample was added to the medium with the final concentration of 0.2 mg/mL), and incubated (310.15 K, 5% CO2) for 4 h. Subsequently, the cells were washed with PBS buffer solution, and fixed by paraformaldehyde (4%) for 5 min. With the addition of commercial dyes including Golgi apparatus specific fluorescent dye (NBD C6-Ceramide, NBD), lysosomal dye (LysoSensor Yellow/Blue DND-160, PDMPO), or nuclear dye (DAPI Staining Solution, DAPI), the cells were incubated at 277.15 K for a few minutes (2.5 μM NBD for 30 min, 5 μM PDMPO for 30 min, 10 μg/mL DAPI for 5 min), and washed with PBS buffer solution for several times. Finally, fluorescence images were taken on a laser confocal microscope for GTCDs or CGCDs (λex/em = 458/580–700 nm), NBD (λex/em = 488/520–560 nm), PDMPO (λex/em = 440/520–570 nm), and DAPI (λex/em = 358/420–500 nm).

GTCD uptake process and imaging stability were investigated by using a Nikon Spinning Disk confocal microscope equipped with an on-stage incubation chamber. The digestion and culture procedures were the same as section Subcellular organelle localization. Before imaging for HeLa cells, medium with 0.1 mg/mL GTCDs was added to sample dish. Time-lapse (5 min) recordings were acquired with 40 × or 100 × objectives using an inverted microscope (Nikon Ti2-E) equipped with a prime 95B camera (Teledyne Photometrics).

In this work, Golgi apparatus-targeting orange-emitting GTCDs were prepared with p-phenylenediamine and benzene sulfonamide as reaction precursors at 453.15 K for 8 h. GTCDs were characterized by a variety of analytical techniques, and then used for Golgi apparatus targeting imaging (Scheme 1).

First, the structure of GTCDs was characterized. TEM image (Fig. 1 a) shows that GTCDs are spherical and evenly dispersed with an average diameter of 3.36 ± 0.98 nm. HRTEM image in the upper right corner shows that GTCDs have obvious lattice stripes with a crystal plane spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) lattice spacing of graphitic carbon, demonstrating their graphite-like structure [14]. The XRD patterns further characterize the structure of GTCDs (Fig. S1). According to the Bragg equation, the interplanar spacing of GTCDs is 0.36 nm, which is close to the 0.34 nm corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of graphite and illustrative of a certain degree of crystallinity. Raman spectra (Fig. 1b) further confirm the graphitization degree of GTCDs. GTCDs exhibit a disordered (D) band at 1365 cm−1, which is related to the existence of sp3 defect, and a crystallization (G) band at 1571 cm−1, which is related to the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon. The intensity ratio I D/I G of GTCDs characteristic bands is 0.64, indicating the graphitic structure of GTCDs [15].

The composition and surface functional groups of GTCDs were characterized by Zeta potential, FT-IR, and XPS. The Zeta potential diagram of GTCDs shows their positive charge of +13.3 mV on the surface (Fig. 1c), which may be caused by the amino groups on the surface. The presence of amino group also plays a certain role in promoting the targeting effect of Golgi apparatus [11]. In the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1d), the broad band at 3022−3689 cm−1 is assigned to O–H and N–H stretching vibration [16], the band at 2926 cm−1 indicates the C–H stretching vibration [17], and the bands at 1514 cm−1 originate from the antisymmetric stretching vibration of CC, CN, C–S, S–N, and C–N, which may be from benzene sulfonamide [18,19]. The bands at 1049 and 829 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of C–OH and S–O, respectively. The existence of hydroxyl is mainly attributed to the participation of solvent in the reaction process [20,21]. In the FT-IR spectra of CGCDs (Fig. S2), the broad band at 3600−3300 cm−1 is assigned to O–H and N–H stretching vibration [22], the band at 2993 cm−1 indicates C–H stretching vibration [23], Compared with GTCDs, CGCDs have a few more obvious absorption bands at 3600−3300, 2993, 1157, 1049, 862, and 569 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of O–H, C–H, OSO, CS, C–S, and sulfonic groups [24–26,36], respectively, which proves that the surface of CGCDs is modified with sulfonic acid residues from benzene sulfonic acid.

XPS spectra were employed to evaluate the elemental composition of GTCDs, which mainly consist of C, N, O, and S elements (H element cannot be detected by XPS) at the atomic percentages of 46.64%, 27.17%, 23.76%, and 2.42%, respectively (Fig. 1e). The mass percentages are showed in Table S1. On the other hand, the elemental analysis gives the mass percentages of C (42.92%), N (9.59%), H (4.16%), S (14.24%), and O (29.09%). In the two test methods, the contents of N and S elements are different, mainly because element analysis deals with the whole material, while XPS detects surface elements. During the synthesis of CDs, carbon core is formed by carbonization at high temperature and it has different composition with the surface of CDs, resulting in a discrepancy between surface and bulk elements. Specially, N element is more concentrated on the surface of CDs.

The high-resolution spectra of C1s (Fig. 1f) exhibit three peaks at 284.41, 285.11, and 286.07 eV, which are assigned to C–C/CC, C–S/C–N, and C–O/CN, respectively, mainly in the form of C–S/C–N bond [27–29]. In the high-resolution spectrum of N1s (Fig. 1g), the peaks at 398.53, 399.24, 400.43, and 400.73 eV can be attributed to pyridinic N, amino N, pyrrole N, and C–NH2, respectively [30,31]. The characteristic peaks of Ols at 530.53, 531.28, 532.08, 532.88, and 533.68 eV are attributed to –C–OH, CO, C–O–C, O–H, and OC–O, respectively, in the high-resolution energy spectrum (Fig. 1h). Oxygen mainly exists as C–O–C groups [32,33]. The characteristic peaks at 168.46 and 169.74 eV in the S2p high-resolution spectra (Fig. 1i) indicate the presence of C–S/N–S and C−SOx (X = 2, 3, and 4) sulfur-containing groups [34–37]. These results show the existence of amino and sulfonamide groups on the surface of GTCDs.

The above results indicate that GTCDs are of spherical particle structures with a uniform size of about 3.36 nm and a relatively high degree of graphitization. They are positively charged and rich in functional groups on their surface, especially amino and sulfonamide groups.

The optical properties of GTCDs were characterized by UV–Vis and PL. The UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 2 a) show two absorption peaks at 245 and 275 nm, indicating the presence of n-π* transitions for CO bonds [37] and n-π* transitions for CN [38], respectively. A relatively weak absorption at 509 nm corresponds to the n-π* transitions of C–N–C and CO on the surface of GTCDs [21]. GTCDs show excitation-independent fluorescence emission characteristics (Fig. 2b), with the optimum excitation wavelength at 366 nm and emission wavelength at 612 nm (orange-red regions). The optical properties of CGCDs in the control group were also analyzed. In the UV–Vis spectra (Fig. S3), CGCDs have two absorption peaks at 227 and 286 nm, indicating the presence of π-π* transitions [11] and n-π* transitions for C = X (X = O or N) [19], respectively. A relatively weak absorption at 584 nm corresponds to the n-π* transitions of CS on the surface of CGCDs [19]. Compared with GTCDs, CGCDs show an optimum emission wavelength at 620 nm and an optimum excitation wavelength at 552 nm (Fig. S3). Despite the lack of targeting effect, CGCDs can still be used as red-emission probes, which is beneficial to biological imaging.

In fluorescent biological imaging, it is necessary for a fluorescent probe to have photobleaching resistance to prevent the sharp decline of fluorescence intensity caused by laser irradiation. To test the anti-bleaching performance of GTCDs, GTCD solution was continuously irradiated for 60 min, and the results show that the fluorescence intensity is only decreased by 8.6% (Fig. 2c), which could be applicable to Golgi apparatus imaging applications. To further verify the light stability of GTCDs, the fluorescence intensity of GTCD aqueous solution at different pH (2–13) was tested. When the pH is at 5–8, the fluorescence intensity is relatively stable with a slight decrease by 3.1% (Fig. 2d), which could be tolerable for fluorescence testing in normal cells and tumour cells with different pH values.

For the sake of developing the bioimaging application of GTCD fluorescent probe, the biosafety of GTCDs was evaluated. The cytotoxicity was primarily investigated. CCK-8 assay shows that the cell survival rate is still higher than 80% when the concentration of GTCDs in the medium even reaches 200 μg/mL (Fig. S4), showing low cytotoxicity and good suitability for cell imaging [39].

Further, the metabolism of GTCDs in vivo was investigated to evaluate their biosafety. A 2.5 mg/mL GTCD solution was prepared for tail intravenous injection in BALB/c nude mice. The experiment suggests that with the extension of injection time, the fluorescence in vivo increases, and almost disappears after 24 h (Fig. S5). It is confirmed that GTCDs have a fast metabolic rate in vivo.

To ulteriorly investigate the metabolic pathway of GTCDs in vivo, BALB/c mice were also injected through caudal vein and euthanized after injection at different times. Organs such as heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney were collected, washed with PBS, and soaked in formalin fixation solution. The results of small animal imaging experiments show that GTCDs mainly exist in the liver and kidney organs of mice, as shown in Fig. 3 .

GTCDs are mainly excreted through urine and feces [40]. Once injected into the blood through the tail vein, some of GTCDs enter the kidney and are quickly excreted through urine within 15 min. In this metabolic pathway test, each time point corresponds to a mouse, so the weak fluorescence in the kidney of a mouse at 4 h may be due to individual difference.

On the other hand, some of GTCDs enter the liver and gradually accumulate. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of the liver is the strongest at 2 h, demonstrating GTCDs reach the liver and could be metabolized through the liver [41,42] (Fig. 3). To further prove that GTCDs can be metabolized through urine, the mice urine was collected in different time periods. It was found that the dark urine with the color of GTCDs was excreted immediately after injection. With the extension of time, the urine gradually resumed its light yellow color as before injection (Fig. S6). The UV–Vis absorption test was carried out on the urine collected at different times, and the absorbance gradually decreased with the extension of time. Therefore, GTCDs circulate in the blood to reach the kidney and liver, the majority of GTCDs are more likely to be retained short-term in the kidney and liver for minutes to hours, and then they can be further metabolized by urine and feces.

The results in Fig. 3 show that the metabolic pathways of GTCDs in vivo are mainly liver and kidney metabolism, and the time of metabolism is about 24 h, which corresponds well with the metabolic time of imaging in vivo. It indicates that GTCDs have the performance of fast metabolism, low toxicity, and low side effects. The rapid metabolism is mainly attributed to the small size of GTCDs, with an average particle size of only 3.36 nm, which makes them be quickly excreted and metabolized through the kidney [36]. In addition, histopathological analysis shows that the cells of each tissue are in normal state (Fig. 4 ), and no tissue lesions are observed. The biosafety results above demonstrate that GTCDs are a low toxicity and fast metabolism Golgi apparatus imaging probe, and have a good biological application prospect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultrafast Golgi apparatus imaging by GTCDs
ce:section.ce:para: GTCDs were used for fluorescence imaging in HeLa cells because of their excellent optical and biological properties. After co-incubation of GTCDs and HeLa for 4 h, GTCDs selectively aggregate in cells, and the Pearson co-localization coefficient of GTCDs and NBD is found to be 0.97. Compared with 0.84 [10] and 0.94 [9] reported in the literature, this value indicates the good ability of GTCDs for Golgi apparatus targeting (Fig. 5 a–e). The Golgi apparatus localization effect of GTCDs in different cells was further tested, as shown in Figs. S7 and S8. The laser confocal imaging results show the Golgi apparatus localization effect of GTCDs in both normal cells (HL-7702 and HSF cells) and cancer cells. Although GTCDs also have Golgi apparatus-targeting effect in normal cells, the fluorescence intensity is weak, which may be due to the presence of a few COX-2 in normal cells. In order to further explore the imaging differentiation of GTCDs in normal cells and tumor cells, the fluorescence intensity of different cells is compared. In Fig. S9, the fluorescence intensity is the strongest in HeLa cells, followed by HepG2, and the fluorescence of HSF and HL-7702 is weak. However, the sample base is not large enough to conclude the rule that GTCDs could distinguish normal cells from tumor cells. Therefore, GTCDs will not be used to distinguish normal cells from tumor cells temporarily.

To further test the specific targeting performance of GTCDs, the localization effects of GTCDs in different organelles (lysosome, Golgi apparatus, and nucleus) were compared (Fig. 6 ). The average Pearson's co-localization coefficients of GTCDs for lysosome, Golgi apparatus, and nucleus are 0.59, 0.92, and 0.32, respectively. These results prove that the lysosome and nucleus show poor fluorescence colocalization effects compared with Golgi apparatus. Therefore, it can be confirmed that GTCDs have better Golgi apparatus targeting effect.

To evaluate the imaging and stability of GTCDs, the endocytosis time was investigated on a living cell workstation. From the characterization of living cell workstation, it is obvious that GTCDs can quickly enter the cell and selectively gather in Golgi apparatus immediately after their addition and have the ability of long-term imaging for 200 min (Fig. 7 ). The comparison with other researches confirms that GTCDs can achieve ultra-fast imaging of Golgi apparatus (Table S2) [16,39,43–46]. The dynamics of GTCDs in HeLa cells within 200 min was recorded at an interval of 5 min, during which no significant cytotoxicity is observed. Under continuous laser irradiation, the cells do not show apoptosis and necrosis (Videos S1 and S2), which proves that GTCDs have good photostability, anti-photobleaching, and low phototoxicity, laying a foundation for their further biological application.
Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2022.110213.

The following are the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1
Multimedia component 2 Multimedia component 2

Kurumbail et al. considered that benzene sulfonamide moiety can target Golgi apparatus on the specific active site of COX-2 [13]. In order to rationalize this reason for the Golgi apparatus targeting of GTCDs synthesized from benzene sulfonamide, the Golgi apparatus-targeting mechanism of GTCDs is analyzed through the functional group control trial, cell control experiment, and binding energy simulation calculation.

The necessity of targeting Golgi apparatus with sulfonamide groups was chiefly verified. With GTCDs containing sulfonamide groups as the experimental group and CGCDs containing sulfonic groups as the control group, the effect of sulfonamide group on Golgi apparatus targeting was verified by changing the type of surface group. The Golgi apparatus-targeting ability of GTCDs and CGCDs was evaluated, as shown in Fig. 5, in which a high linear correlation exists between GTCDs and NBD. The co-localization scatter image of CGCDs control group and NBD shows a double tail bifurcation shape, which proves that the targeting ability is poor (Pearson coefficient = 0.66). As hydrogen bonds are easily formed by the hydrogen atoms located among electronegative atoms, and sulfonamide group has two –NH and more binding sites than sulfonate group, it is speculated that sulfonamide has stronger targeting because it has stronger bonding ability to form hydrogen bonds. CGCDs only replace sulfonamide with sulfonic acid while retaining benzene rings and amino groups, which enables CGCDs to show weak Golgi apparatus-targeting ability through π-π stacking interaction between benzene ring core and amino acid residue.

Because COX-2 inhibitors such as celecoxib (also rich in sulfonamide groups) can selectively target COX-2 in the cells [47], we attempt to conduct experimental validations by using celecoxib as raw materials to synthesize a new fluorescent probe. The targeting component is expected to be inherited from celecoxib to the fluorescent probe, and then the targeting mechanism could be explained by its targeting imaging measurement. Therefore, the control group CGCDs-2 was synthesized by using celecoxib instead of benzene sulfonamide at the same reaction condition with CGCDs. CGCDs-2 also has a similar targeting effect, and a good positioning effect with NBD (as shown in Fig. S10). It is indirectly confirmed that GTCDs target to Golgi apparatus through the interaction between sulfonamide group and COX-2. These control trials reveal that the sulfonamide group on the surface of GTCDs play a paramount role in Golgi apparatus-targeting.

In order to reveal the mechanism of GTCDs targeting Golgi apparatus, the structure of GTCDs was simulated and constructed, and their binding energy was simulated and analyzed by AutoDock. First, the binding energies of benzene sulfonamide and p-toluene sulfonic acid with COX-2 were calculated by simulation, and the targeting performance of the precursor molecules was analyzed. It can be seen from the binding energies of docking molecules in the active site of the enzyme: Benzene sulfonamide (−22.22 kJ/mol) shows better binding effect to COX-2 than p-toluene sulfonate (−20.13 kJ/mol) (Fig. 8 a and b and Table 1 ). As shown in Fig. 9 c and d, there are three main types of docking bonding mode between benzene sulfonamide and COX-2: hydrogen bond, π-σ conjugation, and π-alkyl conjugation. The number of each bonding mode is different: four hydrogen bonds, one π-σ conjugation, and one π-alkyl conjugation. The residue CO of GLN178 and LEU338 and the residue –NH of benzene sulfonamide form hydrogen bond CO… HN. The residue –NH of ARG499 and PHE504 and SO of benzene sulfonamide form hydrogen bond, while the VAL509 residue (−CH3) and the benzene ring core of benzene sulfonamide form π-σ conjugations, and the LEU338 residue (C–C–C) and the benzene ring core of benzene sulfonamide form π-alkyl conjugations. The simulation of the precursor molecules proves that benzene sulfonamide is mainly bound to COX-2 through hydrogen bonding, with a better selectivity compared with p-toluene sulfonate. Therefore, it could be used as the precursor of targeting-functional group to pass the sulfonamide groups on the surface of GTCDs and achieve Golgi apparatus-targeting.

On the basis of the composition of surface functional groups, morphology, and structure characterization of GTCDs (Fig. 1), a model for GTCDs was constructed and optimized. The simulation molecules with abundant functional groups such as –NH2, –OH, and sulfonamide groups were constructed. The size is 2.43 Å for a benzene ring, 1.43 Å for C–S, 1.64 Å for N–S, 1.82 Å for S–O, 1.50 Å for C–N, and 1.51 Å for N–H. Therefore, combined with the characterizations, these data result in a size of about 1.84 nm for GTCDs, which are composed of five horizontal benzene rings and five longitudinal benzene rings with the structure of amino and sulfonamide (Fig. 9a).

The binding energy of simulation molecule of GTCDs and COX-2 was simulated by AutoDock. The results in Fig. 9b show that GTCDs bind to COX-2 in the cavity. The total binding energy of GTCDs and COX-2 is −40.71 kJ/mol. There are seven types of docking binding modes: hydrogen bonds, C–H bonds, and other molecular interactions (π-cation, π-anion, π-S, π-π T type, and π-alkyl conjugation), among which hydrogen bond is the dominant. The residue CO of VAL141 and GLU443 forms CO…HN hydrogen bonds with –NH of sulfonamide, residue CO of VAL118 forms hydrogen bond with –OH on the surface of GTCDs, the residue –OH of ASP143 forms hydrogen bond with –NH around GTCDs, and the residue benzene ring center core of TYR122 forms π-S interaction with sulfonamide of GTCDs. The residue –OH of ASP143 forms π-anion interaction with multiple benzene ring cores of GTCDs, and the HIS119 residue cyclopentadiene group center forms π-π T type interaction with the benzene ring cores of GTCDs (π-electron cloud interaction exists between two aryl groups, but the interaction is T-shaped). According to the AutoDock docking analysis, the docking mode of GTCDs and COX-2 is primarily through the hydrogen bonding between the sulfonamide groups on the surface of GTCDs and the amino acid residues (including VAL118, VAL141, ASP143, and GLU443), so the targeting ability is obtained at the active site of COX-2.

To further verify the effect of sulfonamide groups on targeting, the structure of CGCDs was simulated and constructed, with replacement of the sulfonamide groups on GTCDs by benzene sulfonic acid group (Fig. 9d). The total binding energy of CGCDs and COX-2 is −35.94 kJ/mol. The binding mode of CGCDs with COX-2 is mainly π-π stacking, which explains the slightly higher binding energy of CGCDs than that of GTCDs, and indicates that the lower selective binding ability of p-toluene sulfonic acid with COX-2 than benzene sulfonamide. This confirms the hypothesis that the Golgi apparatus-targeting ability of GTCDs is mainly attributed to the selective binding of surface sulfonamide groups to the highly expressed COX-2 by hydrogen bonding.

The experimental results in section Ultrafast Golgi apparatus imaging by GTCDs have shown that GTCDs have excellent Golgi apparatus-targeting performance, as they can quickly locate the Golgi apparatus immediately after their addition, with an average Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.92. Furthermore, the mechanism of GTCDs targeting the Golgi apparatus was explored through the aforementioned functional group comparison experiments, cell comparison experiments, and binding energy simulation calculations, that is, the relationship between the functional group structure of GTCDs and the Golgi apparatus targeting ability (Fig. 10 ).

First of all, the cell membrane surface is negatively charged, and the fluorescent probe GTCDs are positively charged, which lays the foundation for GTCDs to enter cells quickly (Fig. 10). Second, GTCDs have abundant basic ligand –NH2 and targeting ligand sulfonamide groups on their surface. After entering the cell, they tend to approach the weak acidic environments of the Golgi apparatus and COX-2. The sulfonamide group on the surface actively targets and binds with COX-2 through hydrogen bonds, so that GTCDs are smoothly located in the Golgi apparatus, and accurate targeting imaging of the Golgi apparatus is achieved (Fig. 10a). Finally, from the AutoDock simulations, benzene sulfonamide molecule has stronger hydrogen bond effect, which makes it easier to combine with COX-2 than p-toluene sulfonic acid. In the simulated GTCDs and CGCDs molecules, the sulfonamide structure on GTCDs makes it easier for GTCDs and COX-2 to form hydrogen bonds. With a binding energy slightly lower than the π-π stacking between CGCDs and COX-2 (Fig. 10b). Consequently, GTCDs bind easily with COX-2 for targeting Golgi apparatus.

In brief, the main reason for the rapid and accurate targeting of GTCDs to the Golgi apparatus is that the sulfonamide portion on the surface of GTCDs selectively binds to the expressed COX-2 through hydrogen bonding, and that GTCDs are small in size and positively charged, so that GTCDs have the ability to quickly, accurately, and selectively image the Golgi apparatus after entering the cell.

Novel orange-emitting GTCDs exhibit rapid and accurate targeting towards Golgi apparatus by hydrogen bonding with highly expressed COX-2. GTCDs enter the cells and locate the Golgi apparatus immediately after their addition. They have short metabolic time, low toxicity, and good biosafety, and they also achieve a 200 min-long imaging, suggesting a broad application prospect in the field of Golgi apparatus imaging therapy. Moreover, the higher content of COX-2 expressed in tumor cells enables GTCDs to achieve Golgi apparatus targeting imaging. In the co-localization imaging of GTCDs and CGCDs with Golgi apparatus, GTCDs have a higher Pearson coefficient (0.92) because of the stronger hydrogen bonding between sulfonamide groups and COX-2 than the π-π interaction for CGCDs with a lower Pearson coefficient (0.66). The simulation of AutoDock further confirms that the Golgi apparatus targeting of GTCDs is mainly caused by the formation of hydrogen bonds between the sulfonamide groups on GTCDs surface and the active site of COX-2 on the Golgi apparatus surface. GTCDs are expected to act as a practical tool for Golgi apparatus imaging therapy.

Xin Zhang: Preparation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Lin Chen, Writing, Reviewing, and Editing. Yingying Wei, Supplementary experiment. Jinglei Du: Investigation, Experimental resources, Shiping Yu: Investigation, Experimental resources. Xuguang Liu: Writing, Reviewing, and Editing. Wei Liu: Supplementary experiment. Yanjun Liu, Writing, Reviewing, and Editing. Yongzhen Yang, Writing, Reviewing, and Editing. Qiang Li: Investigation, Experimental resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is supported by the following research projects: Shanxi-Zheda Institute of Advanced Materials and Chemical Engineering, China (2021SX-FR010); Shanxi Technology Innovation Center for Controlled and Sustained Release of Nano-drugs, China (202104010911026); National Natural Science Foundation of China (51803148); Central Guiding Local Science and Technology Development Fund, China (YDZX20201400001722); the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province, China (201901D211501, 201901D211502, and 201901D111419); and Postgraduate Innovation Project of Shanxi Province, China (2021Y266).
